title: securetools – Development Guide
text: R package providing pre-built security-hardened tool definitions use securer. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-security constraints.

title: securetools – Development Guide
text: Tool factories return securer_tool() objects. fn closure captures config (allow-lists, limits, rate limiter). fn runs parent process. Key files: - R/utils-validate.R – Path validation, size limits, SQL identifier checks - R/utils-rate-limit.R – Per-tool invocation rate limiter - R/tool-*.R – One file per tool factory

title: securetools – Development Guide
code: Rscript -e "devtools::test('.')" Rscript -e "devtools::check('.')" Rscript -e "devtools::document('.')"

title: securetools – Development Guide
text: Use withr::local_tempdir() temp directories Use skip_if_not_installed() optional dependencies Use skip_if_no_session() tests requiring SecureSession Test security rejections (path traversal, injection) well happy paths

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: AI agent reads files, queries databases, calls APIs combines LLM’s reasoning executable tools. reasoning probabilistic shaped inputs, tool LLM can call might called unexpected adversarial ways. Agent frameworks must treat tool access security boundary. securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tools plug directly orchestr agents. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object orchestr’s Agent class converts ellmer tool automatically secure = TRUE. get structural security guarantees (path scoping, parameterized SQL, rate limiting) without writing security code . tools enforce constraints design. examples wire securetools single-agent ReAct loops, multi-agent supervisor graphs, mixed toolkits. tool factory basics, see vignette("securetools"). orchestr fundamentals, see vignette("quickstart", package = "orchestr").

title: Using securetools with Agents
code: library(securetools) library(orchestr) library(ellmer)  # Set your LLM provider API key Sys.setenv(ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "your-key-here")

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: ReAct (Reason + Act) agent standard pattern tool-using LLMs. agent receives task, reasons next, acts calling tool, observes result, repeats final answer. iteration gives LLM another chance call tool. confused manipulated agent can spiral unbounded tool use. securetools makes ReAct loops safe routing every tool call parent-process validation reaches sandbox. LLM reasons freely; actions constrained guarantees tool. following diagram shows execution flow ReAct agent securetools: Pass securetools agent() constructor secure = TRUE tool calls run inside securer sandbox. secure = TRUE, orchestr creates SecureSession behind scenes. securer_tool converted ellmer tool definition executes inside sandbox. Path scoping, AST validation, rate limits still apply parent-process level.
code: ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │                   ReAct Loop                      │   │                                                   │   │   ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌───────────┐   │   │   │          │    │          │    │           │   │   │   │  Reason  │───>│   Act    │───>│  Observe  │   │   │   │  (LLM)   │    │ (tool)   │    │ (result)  │   │   │   │          │    │          │    │           │   │   │   └──────────┘    └────┬─────┘    └─────┬─────┘   │   │        ^               │                │         │   │        │               v                │         │   │        │        ┌──────────────┐        │         │   │        │        │   Validate   │        │         │   │        │        │  (parent R)  │        │         │   │        │        │  - rate limit│        │         │   │        │        │  - allow-list│        │         │   │        │        │  - path check│        │         │   │        │        └──────┬───────┘        │         │   │        │               v                │         │   │        │        ┌──────────────┐        │         │   │        │        │   Execute    │        │         │   │        │        │  (sandbox)   │────────┘         │   │        │        └──────────────┘                  │   │        │                                          │   │        └──────────── loop ────────────────────────┘   │                                                   │   │   Done? ──yes──> Return final answer              │   └───────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ # Create security-scoped tools calc <- tool_calculator() reader <- tool_read_file(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/data")  # Build an agent with tools and secure execution analyst <- agent(   "analyst",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You are a data analyst. Use your tools to answer questions."   ),   tools = list(calc, reader),   secure = TRUE )  # Wrap in a ReAct graph for state management graph <- react_graph(analyst)  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "Read the file sales.csv from the data directory and calculate the total revenue." )))

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: supervisor pattern applies principle least privilege across multiple agents. Specialist workers carry tools need. supervisor agent routes incoming requests appropriate worker without holding direct tool access. worker gets SecureSession, rate limits, allowed directories, domain allow-lists isolated per worker. compromised file worker start making HTTP requests. supervisor sees high-level results, raw tool outputs, limits information leakage across agent boundaries. can also tune constraints per role: generous rate limits data specialist, tight caps file writer. supervisor graph routes tasks specialized worker agents, carrying set tools. supervisor needs tools ; uses route tool supervisor_graph() injects automatically. Workers get SecureSession, isolating allowed directories rate limits per worker.
code: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │                 Supervisor Agent                 │   │           (no tools, routes only)                │   │                                                  │   │    "Read sales.csv, compute mean revenue"        │   │                                                  │   │         ┌──────────┬──────────┐                  │   │         v          v          v                  │   │   ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐   │   │   │   File    │ │  Data    │ │  Research    │   │   │   │ Specialist│ │Specialist│ │  Specialist  │   │   │   │           │ │          │ │              │   │   │   │ read_file │ │calculator│ │  fetch_url   │   │   │   │ write_file│ │ profiler │ │              │   │   │   └───────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────────┘   │   │                                                  │   │   Each worker has its own SecureSession          │   │   with isolated rate limits and allow-lists      │   └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ # Data agent: calculation and profiling data_agent <- agent(   "data_specialist",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You are a data specialist.",       "Use the calculator for arithmetic and the profiler for data summaries."     )   ),   tools = list(     tool_calculator(),     tool_data_profile(max_rows = 50000)   ),   secure = TRUE )  # File agent: reading and writing file_agent <- agent(   "file_specialist",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You are a file specialist.",       "Read and write files as requested.",       "Always specify format = 'auto' when reading."     )   ),   tools = list(     tool_read_file(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/data"),     tool_write_file(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/project/output")   ),   secure = TRUE )  # Supervisor routes between specialists supervisor <- agent(   "supervisor",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = paste(       "You coordinate a team.",       "Route data questions to the data specialist",       "and file operations to the file specialist."     )   ) )  graph <- supervisor_graph(   supervisor = supervisor,   workers = list(     data_specialist = data_agent,     file_specialist = file_agent   ) )  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "Read sales.csv, then calculate the mean of the revenue column." )))

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: Rate limiting matters agent loops LLM controls many iterations occur. ReAct agent misunderstands task might call calculator 500 times trying “verify” answer. research agent fetching URLs might follow links recursively, hammering external API. Without hard caps, loops become runaway execution: wasted tokens, exhausted external rate limits, enormous output overwhelms downstream processing. securetools two rate limiting mechanisms: Lifetime caps (max_calls): total number times tool can invoked across entire session. hit, every subsequent call returns error. backstop runaway loops. Sliding window (max_calls_per_minute): limits burst frequency. Even allow 1000 lifetime calls, restricting 10 per minute prevents overwhelming external services disk /O. rate limit reached, tool returns structured error message LLM (R exception), giving agent chance adjust strategy. might summarize far instead fetching data. limit hit, tool returns error message LLM, can stop adjust approach.
code: # Cap the calculator at 50 calls per agent session calc <- tool_calculator(max_calls = 50)  # URL fetch with both lifetime and per-minute limits fetcher <- tool_fetch_url(   allowed_domains = c("api.github.com"),   max_calls = 100,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )  researcher <- agent(   "researcher",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You fetch data from APIs and analyze results."   ),   tools = list(calc, fetcher),   secure = TRUE )  graph <- react_graph(researcher)

title: Using securetools with Agents
text: agents need capabilities beyond securetools provides directly. can combine securetools factories custom securer::securer_tool() definitions agent. tools run inside secure session share sandbox isolation. Custom tools follow security model securetools factories: fn runs parent process (inside sandbox), orchestr handles ellmer conversion automatically.
code: # A custom tool alongside securetools timestamp_tool <- securer::securer_tool(   name = "timestamp",   description = "Return the current UTC timestamp.",   fn = function() {     format(Sys.time(), tz = "UTC", usetz = TRUE)   },   args = list() )  # Mix custom + securetools assistant <- agent(   "assistant",   chat = chat_anthropic(     system_prompt = "You help with data tasks and can check the current time."   ),   tools = list(     tool_calculator(),     tool_read_file(allowed_dirs = "/path/to/data"),     timestamp_tool   ),   secure = TRUE )  graph <- react_graph(assistant)  result <- graph$invoke(list(messages = list(   "What time is it? Also, what is 2^10?" )))

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: give LLM agent access tools, hand executable capabilities system trained helpful, safe. model can call system() can run arbitrary shell commands. model unrestricted file write can overwrite /etc/passwd plant reverse shell. Raw SQL access lets model DROP TABLE users exfiltrate every row production database. theoretical risks. Research prompt injection adversarial tool use shown LLM agents follow carefully-crafted instructions embedded untrusted data – user-uploaded CSV, web page fetched agent, crafted column name database result. tools safety boundaries, single successful injection compromises host system. securetools provides pre-built tool definitions structural security constraints. tool physically access files outside allowed directories. accepts structured parameters constructs parameterized queries internally. agent never sees raw SQL, raw file paths outside sandbox, unrestricted HTTP access.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions use securer. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-constraints: path scoping, parameterized SQL, domain allow-lists, size limits, rate limiting.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Every securetools call follows execution flow. Validation happens parent process, sandbox influence. Validation runs parent process, sandboxed code modify bypass. Even sandbox escape possible, parent-level checks (path scoping, rate limits, SQL parameterization) still apply execute call reaches sandbox.
code: LLM Agent                 Parent Process (R)              Sandbox (securer)   =========                 ==================              =================       |                            |                               |       |--- tool_call(args) ------->|                               |       |                            |                               |       |                     1. Validate args:                      |       |                        - Check rate limit                  |       |                        - Resolve symlinks                  |       |                        - Match allow-lists                 |       |                        - Verify size limits                |       |                            |                               |       |                     2. Reject? -----> Error to LLM         |       |                            |                               |       |                     3. Pass validated -------> Execute     |       |                        call to sandbox         in sandbox  |       |                            |                               |       |                            |<------------- Result ---------|       |                            |                               |       |<--- result/error ----------|                               |

title: Getting Started with securetools
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ian-flores/securetools")

title: Getting Started with securetools
code: library(securetools) library(securer)

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: LLM asked “calculate” something might generate arbitrary R code instead simple arithmetic expression. Without validation, calculator(expression = "system('rm -rf /')") execute destructive shell command. Code injection expression evaluation well-understood attack vector. calculator tool evaluates mathematical expressions safely via AST validation. parses expression abstract syntax tree walks every node, verifying arithmetic operators, math functions, numeric literals present. Variable access, assignment, arbitrary function calls rejected evaluation ever occurs.
code: calc <- tool_calculator() session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(calc))  session$execute('calculator(expression = "sqrt(144) + 2^3")') #> [1] 20  # This is rejected; system() is not an allowed function: session$execute('calculator(expression = "system(\'whoami\')")') #> Error: Function not allowed in calculator: `system`  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: agent unrestricted file access can read secrets (~/.ssh/id_rsa, .env files, /etc/shadow) write sensitive locations. Path traversal attacks using ../../ sequences symlinks let attacker escape intended directory. agent might also exhaust disk space writing extremely large files loop. File /O tools enforce path scoping symlink resolution, size limits, directory allow-lists. Reading writing scoped independently can give agent read access source data without granting write access directory:
code: # Only allow access to a specific directory data_dir <- "/path/to/project/data"  reader <- tool_read_file(   allowed_dirs = data_dir,   max_file_size = "50MB",   max_rows = 10000 )  writer <- tool_write_file(   allowed_dirs = data_dir,   max_file_size = "10MB",   overwrite = FALSE )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(reader, writer))  # Read a CSV (auto-detects format from extension) session$execute('   data <- read_file(path = "/path/to/project/data/input.csv", format = "auto")   head(data) ')  # Write results (path traversal is blocked) session$execute('   result <- data.frame(x = 1:5, y = letters[1:5])   write_file(     path = "/path/to/project/data/output.csv",     content = result,     format = "csv"   ) ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: SQL injection well-known attack class. LLM given raw SQL interface can tricked (via prompt injection user data fetched content) running DROP TABLE, UNION SELECT data exfiltration, INSERT/UPDATE tamper records. Parameterized queries help, still require developer use correctly. securetools eliminates attack surface entirely: agent never writes SQL . tool_query_sql() exposes structured interface agent specifies table name, column list, optional filter. tool constructs SQL internally using parameterized queries. Injection structurally impossible agent mechanism supply raw SQL strings: table column names validated allow-lists. pre-approved tables can queried, column names checked SQL injection patterns interpolated query.
code: Agent Request                           Tool Internals   =============                           ==============   table = "users"             --->   SELECT name, email   columns = "name, email"           FROM users   filter_column = "active"          WHERE active = ?   filter_value = "1"                 [bound: "1"] library(DBI) con <- dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "app.db")  sql_tool <- tool_query_sql(    conn = con,   allowed_tables = c("users", "orders"),   max_rows = 1000 )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(sql_tool))  # Query with filter (uses parameterized query internally) session$execute('   query_sql(     table = "users",     columns = "name, email",     filter_column = "active",     filter_value = "1"   ) ')  session$close() dbDisconnect(con)

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: agent unrestricted HTTP access can perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), making requests internal services (http://169.254.169.254 cloud metadata, http://localhost:8080 internal APIs) invisible public internet. can also exfiltrate data POSTing attacker-controlled endpoints, overwhelm external APIs unbounded request loops. URL fetch tool constrains network access domain allow-lists (using glob patterns subdomain matching), protocol restrictions (HTTP/HTTPS ), private IP blocking, response size limits, rate limiting:
code: fetcher <- tool_fetch_url(   allowed_domains = c("api.github.com", "*.githubusercontent.com"),   max_response_size = "1MB",   timeout_secs = 30,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(fetcher))  session$execute('   result <- fetch_url(url = "https://api.github.com/zen", method = "GET")   result$body ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: profiling enormous datasets without limits can exhaust memory crash host process. tool_data_profile() enforces row limits sampling, multi-million-row data frames summarized safely without consuming available memory. Compute summary statistics data frames:
code: profiler <- tool_data_profile(max_rows = 100000) session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(profiler))  session$execute('   profile <- data_profile(data = iris)   profile$columns[[1]]  # Sepal.Length stats ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: R’s plotting system powerful enough execute arbitrary code. expression like plot(x); system("curl evil.com") embeds system call inside looks like plotting code. Without restrictions, plot tool becomes general-purpose code execution backdoor. plot tool evaluates plot expressions restricted environment safe base R graphics functions available. Output also path-scoped size-limited prevent writing oversized files arbitrary locations:
code: plotter <- tool_plot(   allowed_dirs = "/path/to/output",   max_file_size = "5MB" )  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(plotter))  session$execute('   plot(     path = "/path/to/output/chart.png",     plot_code = "hist(rnorm(1000), main = \"Distribution\")",     width = 8,     height = 6   ) ')  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: threat: unrestricted documentation lookup might seem harmless, can leak information installed packages system configuration. allowed_packages parameter restricts packages can queried, keeping agent’s awareness scoped intend. tool_r_help() gives safe access R documentation:
code: help_tool <- tool_r_help(allowed_packages = c("base", "stats", "utils"))  session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(help_tool))  # Look up documentation for a function result <- session$call("r_help", topic = "mean", package = "base") cat(result)  session$close()

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Every tool enforces constraints parent process level, sandbox. means even sandbox somehow bypassed, tool-level protections still hold: Path scoping: symlinks resolved checking allowed directories Parameterized SQL: structured interface prevents injection design Domain allow-lists: URL validation HTTP request Size limits: checked serialization (write) processing (read) Rate limiting: per-tool lifetime /sliding window limits AST validation: calculator expression safety via recursive AST walking

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: securetools protects untrusted LLM agent attempting : Read sensitive files: path validation symlink resolution prevents directory traversal Write arbitrary locations: write paths validated allowed directories Execute arbitrary code: calculator uses AST validation; plot tool restricts plotting functions Access internal services (SSRF): URL fetching validates protocols (HTTP/HTTPS ) blocks private IPs SQL injection: structured query interface parameterized filters prevents raw SQL injection Resource exhaustion: rate limiting size limits constrain resource usage

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: TOCTOU races: path validation file /O, symlink theoretically swapped. Use validate_written_path() post-write verification. Rate limiting per-process: limits reset R process restarts. persistent rate limiting, use external mechanism. DNS rebinding: private IP check resolves DNS request, DNS rebinding attacks bypass . Base R access restricted environments: plot tool’s restricted environment still provides access safe base R functions. allowlist comprehensive may need updates new R versions.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: tools called SecureSession IPC, tool arguments must specified explicitly. child process wrappers carry default values tool factory. example, always specify format = "auto" even though ’s default direct usage.

title: Getting Started with securetools
text: Agent loops unbounded default. ReAct agent keeps calling tools decides answer hits token limit. Without rate limiting, confused manipulated agent make thousands API calls, exhaust disk /O, run cloud costs. Rate limits impose hard boundary independent LLM’s decision-making. tool factories accept max_calls lifetime rate limiting. tools (like tool_fetch_url()) also support max_calls_per_minute sliding-window throttling. limit hit, tool returns error message LLM rather silently failing, giving agent chance adjust strategy:
code: # Allow only 100 calculator evaluations per session calc <- tool_calculator(max_calls = 100)  # URL fetch with both lifetime and per-minute limits fetcher <- tool_fetch_url(   max_calls = 1000,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for Large Language Model Agents
text: LLMs can call system(), write path, run arbitrary SQL. securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool wrappers enforce sandboxing, path restrictions, query validation – can give AI agents real capabilities without giving keys kingdom.

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for Large Language Model Agents
text: securetools part 7-package ecosystem building governed AI agents R: securetools provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions plug directly securer sessions. sits middle layer alongside secureguard securecontext, giving agents safe access files, SQL, URLs, computation.

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for Large Language Model Agents
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ian-flores/securetools")

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for Large Language Model Agents
code: library(securetools) library(securer)  # Create tools with security constraints calc <- tool_calculator() reader <- tool_read_file(allowed_dirs = "/data", max_file_size = "50MB") sql <- tool_query_sql(conn = con, allowed_tables = c("users", "orders"))  # Use with SecureSession session <- SecureSession$new(tools = list(calc, reader, sql)) session$execute('calculator(expression = "sqrt(144) + 2^3")') #> [1] 20 session$close()

title: Security-Hardened Tool Library for Large Language Model Agents
text: Factory functions: tool_read_file(allowed_dirs = "/data") forces explicit security configuration Parent-side enforcement: Tool constraints run host process, sandbox. Even sandbox bypass leaves tool protections intact raw SQL: Structured query interface makes injection structurally impossible Symlink resolution: normalizePath() path prefix check prevents symlink escape AST walking: Calculator validates expression trees, strings

what: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
text: Composes tool securer::securer_tool() guardrails secureguard. returned object securer::securer_tool() (schema, IPC contract) whose closure runs invocation supplied input guardrails, executes underlying tool, runs result output guardrails.

title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
code: guarded_tool(tool, input_guards = list(), output_guards = list())

title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
text: tool securer_tool object (typically one tool_*() factories package, securer_tool works). input_guards list secureguard input guardrails (type "input" "code"). receives stringified tool args must pass call proceed. output_guards list secureguard output guardrails (type "output"). receives tool's return value (coerced text via secureguard::output_to_text) must pass result returned.

title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
text: new securer_tool object guardrails applied.

title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
text: Guardrail failures translated errors raised tool closure; inside securer session surface tool-call errors returned LLM via ellmer's ContentToolResult(error =) shape. Guardrails applied lazily: secureguard installed, calling guarded_tool() errors clear installation hint rather silently skipping enforcement.

title: Wrap a securer_tool with input and output guardrails — guarded_tool
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   calc <- tool_calculator()   injection <- secureguard::guard_prompt_injection()   secrets <- secureguard::guard_output_secrets(action = "block")   guarded <- guarded_tool(     calc,     input_guards = list(injection),     output_guards = list(secrets)   ) } # }

title: securetools: Security-Hardened Tool Definitions for securer — securetools-package
text: Provides pre-built, security-hardened tool definitions use securer package. tool factory returns securer::securer_tool() object built-security constraints path validation, allow-lists, rate limiting, parameterized queries.

title: securetools: Security-Hardened Tool Definitions for securer — securetools-package
text: Maintainer: Ian Flores Siaca iflores.siaca@hey.com [copyright holder]

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() evaluates mathematical expressions safely via AST validation.

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
code: tool_calculator(max_calls = NULL)

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
text: max_calls Maximum number invocations allowed. NULL (default) means unlimited.

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
text: calculator tool evaluates mathematical expressions restricted environment. following functions operators allowed: Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, ^, %%, %/% Math: sqrt, abs, log, log2, log10, exp, ceiling, floor, round, trunc Trigonometry: sin, cos, tan, asin, acos, atan Aggregation: sum, mean, max, min, length Utilities: c, pi Expressions first parsed validated via AST walk rejects function call symbol allowlist. Evaluation occurs minimal environment containing allowed functions, emptyenv() parent prevent access R functionality.

title: Create a calculator tool — tool_calculator
code: # \donttest{ calc <- tool_calculator() # Basic arithmetic calc@fn(expression = "2 + 3 * 4") #> [1] 14  # Math functions calc@fn(expression = "sqrt(144) + log(exp(1))") #> [1] 13  # With rate limiting calc <- tool_calculator(max_calls = 100) # }

what: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() computes summary statistics data frame.

title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
code: tool_data_profile(max_rows = 1e+05, max_calls = NULL)

title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
text: max_rows Maximum rows profile. Larger data frames sampled. Default 100000. max_calls Maximum invocations allowed. NULL (default) means unlimited.

title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
text: Computes per-column statistics including type, missing count, unique count. numeric integer columns, also computes min, max, mean, median, standard deviation. character factor columns, returns top 5 frequent values counts. input data frame exceeds max_rows, random sample max_rows rows profiled result indicates sampling occurred. data argument declared type "list" tool schema IPC serialization layer converts data frames lists. tool automatically coerces list input back data frame.

title: Create a data profiling tool — tool_data_profile
code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_data_profile(max_rows = 50000, max_calls = 10) # }

title: Create a URL fetch tool — tool_fetch_url
code: tool_fetch_url(   allowed_domains,   max_response_size = "1MB",   timeout_secs = 30,   max_calls = NULL,   max_calls_per_minute = 10 )

title: Create a URL fetch tool — tool_fetch_url
text: allowed_domains Character vector allowed domains (required). Use *.example.com wildcard subdomains matching subdomain bare domain . max_response_size Maximum response body size. Default "1MB". timeout_secs Request timeout seconds. Default 30. max_calls Maximum lifetime invocations. NULL means unlimited. max_calls_per_minute Maximum invocations per 60-second window. Default 10.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — tool_fetch_url
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — tool_fetch_url
text: tool enforces several layers security: Protocol restriction: http https schemes accepted. protocols (e.g. file://, ftp://) rejected. Private IP blocking: Hostnames resolve private reserved IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x, 0.0.0.0) blocked prevent SSRF attacks. redirect following: HTTP redirects followed, preventing redirect-based SSRF bypasses. Domain allow-list: Every request checked allowed_domains list. Wildcard entries like *.example.com match subdomain (e.g. api.example.com, deep.sub.example.com) bare example.com. Curl-level size limit: maxfilesize curl option caps download max_response_size bytes, additional post-download nchar check backup. Rate limiting: per-minute lifetime invocation limits enforced.

title: Create a URL fetch tool — tool_fetch_url
code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_fetch_url(   allowed_domains = c("api.example.com", "*.cdn.example.com"),   max_response_size = "512KB",   timeout_secs = 10 ) # }

what: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() evaluates R plotting code saves result file.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
code: tool_plot(   allowed_dirs,   default_width = 8,   default_height = 6,   max_file_size = "5MB",   max_calls = NULL,   default_dpi = 150 )

title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can write . default_width Default plot width inches. Default 8. default_height Default plot height inches. Default 6. max_file_size Maximum output file size. Default "5MB". max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited. default_dpi Default resolution dots per inch raster formats (png, jpg). Default 150.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
text: plot tool evaluates R plotting code restricted environment. evaluation, AST walk validates allowed functions called, preventing arbitrary code execution. following categories functions permitted: Graphics: plot, lines, points, abline, hist, barplot, boxplot, curve, title, legend, axis, mtext, text, par, grid, segments, arrows, polygon, rect, symbols, pie, pairs, heatmap, image, contour, persp, stripchart, dotchart, stars, sunflowerplot, coplot, cdplot, fourfoldplot, mosaicplot, assocplot, smoothScatter, spineplot, stem Helpers: mathematical functions (sqrt, log, exp, etc.), string functions (paste, sprintf, etc.), statistical distributions (dnorm, rnorm, etc.) Data manipulation: data.frame, list, matrix, lapply, sapply, subset, , others Operators: arithmetic, comparison, logical operators Flow control: , , , {, assignment Supported output formats: png, pdf, svg, jpg/jpeg. format auto-detected file extension default.

title: Create a plot rendering tool — tool_plot
code: # \donttest{ plt <- tool_plot(allowed_dirs = tempdir()) # Basic scatter plot plt@fn(   path = file.path(tempdir(), "scatter.png"),   plot_code = "plot(1:10, (1:10)^2, main = 'Example')" ) #> $path #> [1] "/tmp/RtmpgVTReK/scatter.png" #>  #> $size #> [1] 27235 #>  #> $format #> [1] "png" #>   # With custom dimensions and DPI plt <- tool_plot(   allowed_dirs = tempdir(),   default_width = 10,   default_height = 8,   default_dpi = 300 ) # }

what: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() queries database tables via structured interface parameterized queries. raw SQL accepted – makes SQL injection structurally impossible.

title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
code: tool_query_sql(conn, allowed_tables, max_rows = 1000, max_calls = NULL)

title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
text: conn DBI connection object. allowed_tables Character vector table names tool can query. max_rows Maximum rows returned. Default 1000. max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited.

title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
text: Security constraints: Structured SELECT : tool constructs SELECT queries structured arguments. raw SQL accepted, making SQL injection structurally impossible. Parameterized filters: Filter values passed query parameters, never interpolated SQL strings. Identifier quoting: Table column names quoted DBI::dbQuoteIdentifier() passing allow-list validation, providing defense depth. Table allow-list: tables listed allowed_tables can queried.

title: Create a SQL query tool — tool_query_sql
code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_query_sql(   conn = DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), ":memory:"),   allowed_tables = c("customers", "orders"),   max_rows = 500 ) # }

title: Create an R help documentation tool — tool_r_help
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create an R help documentation tool — tool_r_help
text: tool restricts documentation lookup packages specified allowed_packages. topic name package name must provided; package must allow-list. Help text rendered plain text via tools::Rd2txt() truncated max_lines lines.

code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_r_help(   allowed_packages = c("base", "stats", "utils"),   max_lines = 200 ) # }

what: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() reads files specified directories format detection size limits.

title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
code: tool_read_file(   allowed_dirs,   max_file_size = "50MB",   max_rows = 10000,   max_calls = NULL )

title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can read . max_file_size Max file size. Default "50MB". Accepts bytes string like "10MB". max_rows Maximum rows tabular formats. Default 10000. max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited.

title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
text: Supported formats: csv, json, txt, xlsx, parquet, rds. Format detected automatically file extension, can specified explicitly via format argument. Security measures: Path validation: paths resolved via base::normalizePath() checked allowed_dirs. Symlinks resolved directory check, preventing symlink-based escapes. RDS sandboxing: RDS files deserialized separate subprocess via callr, isolating main process malicious objects execute code load. Size limits: Files exceeding max_file_size rejected reading. Row limits: Tabular formats (csv, xlsx) capped max_rows rows.

title: Create a file reading tool — tool_read_file
code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_read_file(   allowed_dirs = c("/data/reports", "/data/exports"),   max_file_size = "10MB",   max_rows = 5000 ) # }

what: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
text: Returns securer::securer_tool() writes data files specified directories size limits overwrite protection.

title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
code: tool_write_file(   allowed_dirs,   max_file_size = "10MB",   max_calls = NULL,   overwrite = FALSE )

title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
text: allowed_dirs Character vector directories tool can write . max_file_size Max output file size. Default "10MB". max_calls Maximum invocations. NULL means unlimited. overwrite Whether allow overwriting existing files. Default FALSE.

title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
text: securer_tool object.

title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
text: content argument type declared "list" tool schema IPC serialization layer (JSON) converts R objects lists. practice, callers pass: data.frame CSV JSON formats character vector TXT format R object RDS format Supported formats: csv, json, txt, rds. Format auto-detected file extension, can specified explicitly. Security constraints: Atomic writes: Data written temp file first, validated size, moved target path. Overwrite protection: default, existing files overwritten (controlled overwrite parameter). Symlink resolution: Target paths resolved via base::normalizePath() prevent symlink-based directory escapes. Size limits: Written files exceeding max_file_size rejected moved target.

title: Create a file writing tool — tool_write_file
code: # \donttest{ tool <- tool_write_file(   allowed_dirs = "/data/exports",   max_file_size = "5MB",   overwrite = FALSE ) # }

title: Pipe-friendly alias for guarded_tool() — with_guards
text: Lets write tool_calculator() |> with_guards(input_guards = list(...)).

title: Pipe-friendly alias for guarded_tool() — with_guards
code: with_guards(tool, ...)

title: Pipe-friendly alias for guarded_tool() — with_guards
text: tool guarded_tool(). ... Passed straight guarded_tool().

title: Pipe-friendly alias for guarded_tool() — with_guards
text: new securer_tool object.

title: securetools 0.2.0
text: Removed deprecated *_tool() aliases: calculator_tool(), query_sql_tool(), read_file_tool(), write_file_tool(), fetch_url_tool(), plot_tool(), data_profile_tool(), r_help_tool(). Use tool_*() factories introduced 0.1.0 instead. old names warning since 0.1.0.

title: securetools 0.2.0
text: guarded_tool(tool, input_guards, output_guards) — compose securer_tool  input/output guardrails. Returns drop-securer_tool replacement enforces guardrails every invocation surfaces failures tool-call errors. with_guards() — pipe-friendly alias guarded_tool().

title: securetools 0.2.0
text: lifecycle dropped Imports (deprecation duology removed). secureguard (>= 0.3.0) added Suggests new adapter. Minimum securer bumped 0.2.0.

title: securetools 0.1.0
text: read_file_tool() – Sandboxed file reading path validation write_file_tool() – Sandboxed file writing size limits calculator_tool() – Safe expression evaluation AST validation query_sql_tool() – Structured SQL queries parameterized filters fetch_url_tool() – HTTP fetching domain allowlists SSRF protection plot_tool() – Plot generation restricted code evaluation data_profile_tool() – Dataset profiling summary statistics r_help_tool() – R documentation lookup package restrictions Internal validation utilities path safety, SQL identifiers, rate limiting Comprehensive test suite covering security rejection paths Vignette usage examples security model documentation
